VerbalDescription: Copy the input then color in the squares on the outside so that the shape is over the other
Change the two blue squares in between the grey squares into grey.

Change the grid to the input size. There will be two objects of different colors on the grid. One will appear to be laying on top of the other. You need to switch the overlaid colors to the opposite color so that the object that was underneath is now on top. Keep the colors of the individual objects.
Copy your grid and objects exactly. Then bring forward the second color as if bringing a solid object forward.  Keep the colors the same.
Copy the input from the demonstration screen. Redraw the shapes so the shape in the background is in front of the shape in the foreground.
Copy the input from the demonstration grid. redraw the shapes so that the object in the background appears on top of the object in the foreground.
what ever appears behind one color, move it infront.
the regular of color of the very simple. the different risk of mind own games of puzzle.
Keep grid the same, move the layer that is "under" the larger layer to overlay the layer "above" it. Keep colors the same.
Keep the grid size and colors the same. Change the color where the two shapes intersect to the opposite color so it looks like the shape has now moved behind the other shape.

There should be two shapes presented on the input grid. Resize the output grid to the same dimensions as the input grid. Then, your task is to replicate the shapes as shown. However, as I will try and explain through an example: if there's a yellow shape and a green shape (with the green shape covering part of the yellow shape), you need to change it so that the same shapes are there, but the yellow shape is covering part of the green shape, similar to how layers work in photo editing software.
copy grid size from input, move the shape behind to be "above" the other
Shift the shape that's behind the other to in front. Keep the sizes and colours etc the same.
There are two different coloured shapes which overlap with one shape in front and the other behind. Reverse this so the shape in front is now behind and the shape behind is now in front.
Move the shape behind to the foreground. That is, the colours of the background shape should be filled to make it the foreground shape instead.
Move the image on top to the bottom of the other image.  They stay the same place on the grid though.
Copy from input. Imagine you are looking down on colored blocks sitting on top of each other. Re-color the blocks so the bottom block is now on the top.
copy the size of the grid from the original. where the blocks overlap invert the colours
Change around the overlay of the figures. Bring the back figures to front.

Make the grid the same height and width as the original. The shapes will go in the same exact location as the original. Start with the same colors in the same spaces.  Think of the colors as two ribbons. The color that appears to be "under" the other color will need to be changed to the color that is "on top of" the other color, so you will be changing the colors of the blocks that appear to be "layered" to the other color than the original.

There will be a long shape that seems to have bars on it, and another shape over top of it with a color outlining it and another color filling it. Using the same dimensions and idea, copy the original image. From there, make the multicolored shape go from appearing in front of the other figure to appear to be behind it.
move the background object to the front

Take the smaller colored group and put it behind the bigger. You want to color in the blocks of the one in front with the colors behind it, so it looks like the block is behind the bigger group instead of in front of it.
Same grid. Change the foreground piece into a background piece.
the predominant object supercede the other and takes the full form
The smaller overlapping shape moves behind the larger shape.
You want to change the color of any blocks from the small shape to match the object squares of the big shape where the small shape overlaps the large shape.
Transform the image, and make the smaller shape go behind the larger shape.
Copy grid size from input. Do not change shapes size or color. Switch which shape is on the top and bottom layer.

the grid size needs to be the exact same size as the input.  the two objects will be in the same places as the input however the small red object needs to appear behind the larger dark red object.  you need to colour the 5 red squares which share the same grid squares as the large object the darker red.  the result will be the same two objects but the smaller one only consists of 4 red squares and now looks like it is behind the larger object
Move the object that is in front of the other one to the back so that the one that was behind is in front. Everything else stays the same.

Use the same grid size as the input. You should see two shapes - a rectangular shape (perhaps with several colours) and a ladder-like shape. The rectangular shape should be in the foreground (in front of the ladder), and the ladder shape should be in the background (behind the rectangle). You should copy these shapes identically, except the ladder shape should be in FRONT of the rectangle shape (ie. the ladder is the foreground, and the rectangle is the background).

There are two objects in the soluton. Use same grid size as the input. Use same objects color and position from the input, but the smaller objects color should never overlap the color of the larger object. Black grid space is not apart of the larger object.

You're presented with two shapes overlapping one another. The rule is to put the outermost shape behind the barred shape so you almost "imprison" it. You do this by looking at the "cell" color and place it where the two intersect I.E. where they clash.

You'll have two shapes intersecting: One rectangle and another behind it that look like jail cells. The rule is where the cell meets the shape, place its colors in the spaces where the two meet so it looks like the cell shape is in the foreground over the rectangle
Copy it. Then, there should be a big object made up of bars, and a smaller object somewhere on it. You want to make the smaller object appear to be behind the bars instead of in front.
Move the forefront shape behind the background shape.  It will make it look like it is behind it now instead of on top.

Make sure you copy the grid size and everything in it.  So now basically you'll see a small rectangle with another rectangle in side of it somewhere on the grid. This will appear to be on top of a larger shape that looks like a ladder. You want to make it look like the ladder is on top of the two rectangles.  So you will do this by using the color of the ladder and filling out the points where the ladder connects.  You should not add any black squares because again it's going to look as if the ladder is on top now so you would still see the two rectangles "underneath" the ladder.

Change the grid size to the input size. There will be two shapes. One with appear to be on top of the other shape and blocking it. You need to change the appearance so that the lined shaped appears over top of the other shape. Keep the locations of the shapes the same.
Copy the input and make the bigger grate looking shapes lines all go over the smaller shape that is currently on top of it
copy from input- the size, shape and colours. Now colour the blocks to make the shape that looks like it is on top go behind the other shape.

THere will be a grate pattern of one colour with a another coloured shape over the top of it somewhere. Recreate the image with the same colours and grid dimensions, but as if the shape was now instead behind the grate, not in front of it.
Use the same grid size as represented by the task and wherever the two blocks intersects, change the color of the intersecting grid to the other block color.

Copy the original grid using the exact same colors, the only difference is you must remove any vertical lines that go behind the horizontal lines. Vertical lines that go in front of the horizontal line will remain in the same spot.
Vertical lines that go behind the horizontal lines are removed.

Use the same grid size as the input. There will be several lines in the input. Recreate these in your answer in the same positions except for the line which is intersected the most. Keep all the same colours.

A seven by seven grid. There are coloured horizontal lines and colored vertical lines. The vertical lines which pass behind the horizontal lines are to be removed, but the vertical lines which pass in front of the horizontal lines - and the horizontal lines themselves are to remain.

The rule is to completely remove any column that is 'weaving' through the horizontal colored lines and leave any that are a solid-colored column. In this case you are going to copy the grid completely from input and remove the left-most 2 blue columns, leaving the gray horizontal rows intact. When you are done, you will only have the right-most blue column left, along with the 2 original gray rows.

Same grid size as input. Reproduce exact same image as input. You will see 1 or more vertical lines. Leave alone vertical lines that go completely across from top to bottom without interruption. Remove - make black and erase - all vertical lines that alternate with the horizontal lines.
Copy the image exactly.  Consider the vertical lines or shapes shown.  Remove only the coloured line that is shown in the background by changing the squares to black.
The examples showed the more cells with one colour were the dominant colour and the other was wiped out.
i followed the instruction . by cross checking the image .an painting the right portion
With the same size grid, remove any vertical lines that go under the horizontal lines.
Copy test input. Erase any vertically aligned objects that intersect behind any horizontally aligned objects.

One of the object should be removed in the input grid. Based on the task demonstration, the incomplete object (without connected colour) should be removed. Apply the same pattern to the test input grid.
Remove any vertical lines that go "under" the lattice.
Keep the grid the same as the test grid. Remove any vertical bars that appear to go under the horizontal bars.
Using the same size grid as the input, remove any vertical lines that go under the horizontal lines.
The vertical pattern that looks like it goes behind the horizonital lines is removed while the vertical pattern that looks like it goes over the top remains.
Copy the input as it is, if a perpendicular line is under the horizontal line(s), get rid of it (them). If a perpendicular line is COMPLETELY over the line, keep. Same colors.
Make the grid the same size as the input grid. Using the same size, color, and position, draw only the object in the "background" of the input image. Do not add the object on top of it.
Just remove the front object

The grid remains the same. Copy the input over. The gray pattern needs to be removed but by imagining that the blue pattern is underneath it and full. Color the outer gray squares  that extend beyond the blue to black. Color the inner gray squares that are on top of the blue the same light blue as the rectangle.

In the input grid, find the object that is in the background. (The object that is being covered by another object on the grid). This background object is what will be placed in the grid. Resize the grid to reflect the size presented in the first grid. Using the same color as the background object, create the background object in the grid by itself, keeping it in the same location and orientation, only removing the object that was previously covering or "on top of" the background object.

You should see what looks like one shape that is overlapping another. 
For example you could see three vertical blue blocks with one red block either side giving the impression the the blue shape is overlapping the red one. For this solution remove ALL of the blue blocks and fill in the blocks red that seemed to be being covered.
Use the same grid size as in the input. Pick the underlying object and fill in it in the gride. Keep the colour the same as the colour of the underlying object.
Remove the overlaid coloured blocks that protrude vertically over the other coloured blocks.  Fill in any missing blocks to complete a rectangle in the same colour as the other remaining blocks.
Use the same grid size as in the input. Remove the vertical object and fill in the object that is still there. keep it's color the same
Use the same size grid as in the input. Copy the widest object and remove the tallest one.
Remove the shape in the foreground, leaving only the solid shape in the background. Fill in any parts of the foreground that would overlap with the background colour.
Make the grid sizes same as the input. Draw the shape which is in the BOTTOM, same color, same size.
One shape appears to be in front of the other. Copy the input, and remove the shape that is "on top". Now complete the shape beneath, in its proper color.
Figure out which shape is in the foreground and remove it.  Then, complete the remaining shape in the same color.
Grid size and color stay the same. remove the top shape, place just the bottom shape
make a grid the same size as original. Draw only the background shape filling in any squares that are covered by the foreground shape with the background shape color

Copy the original grid with all its elements. Note the two distinct coherent objects of different colors within the grid. Note that one of the objects is covering a portion of the other. Remove that upper layer object so that the bottom object is displayed alone and in full in its original color, with the assumption that the bottom object is not irregularly shaped, ie it is a rectangle or a line.

Maintain the same grid dimensions as the input. Identify the two shapes, assuming they they are both rectangular and any could be overlapping. Take the smaller shape and shift the entire shape one unit to the left. If both shapes are the same size, shift the shape that is lower vertically.
Move the smaller shape one grid square to the left

Keep the grid the same size. Copy the larger grid or if they are the same size, the one on the left will be the same size. For the smaller object or the object on the bottom, move it one space to the left keeping it the same size.
the examples demonstrated the bottom batch of squares moving over to the left by one column
Use the same grid size. Move the shape in front of the other one square to the left. Colours remain the same.

The rule is that the shape on top should move one square to the left. You should recreate the input by clicking on copy inout, which will resize the grid as well. You should then use the edit function to reposition the small magenta shape one square to the left - click on edit, and select the colour you wish to change to, then click where you want the new grid square to be. Once you have repostioned the top grid qare, use the edit function to chang ethe original grid square to the colour of the shape underneath.

Use the same size grid as the input. Select copy from input. Keeping its 2x2 shape, move the red object one cell over to the left. Make sure there is now a singular cell light blue outline around the red object. Fill in the rest of the grid black.

Copy the input exactly. Once you've done that, identify the second rectangle. The second rectangle is the one whose upper left square is further down and/or right than that of the other rectangle. Move the second rectangle one grid space to the left. If the second rectangle overlaps the first in the input, it will continue to overlap (if they still overlap after the move). If the two rectangles do not overlap in the input, but the move will cause them to overlap, then the first rectangle should occlude the second rectangle instead.
Copy grid size. Use same colors. Slide blocks together so  it does not hang over the edge of each other
Copy the size and objects from the input grid. If any of the objects are cubes, move the cube one block to the left.
Keep the same grid size. Keep the larger object in the same spot. Move the smaller object 1 square left. Keep same colour
Using the same size 10x10 grid. Keep the orange object where it is but move the rectangle shape one space to the left (this will mean one less orange block).
move the one farther from the center one block towards the center.
Move the entire shape that is "above" the lower shape to the left by 1 column. Keep colors and grid size the same.
The smaller shape in the image moves over one complete slot to the left.
copy grid from input, then move the shape on the top one square to the left
Take the shape that appears to be on top and move it 1 grid to the left.

Your input should show two shapes one on top of the other (ie one overlapping the other) the one that is on top should be moved 1 grid space to the left. Your submission grid should be the same size as the input and be the same as the input apart from that shape being moved on grid space to the left (ie all colours should stay the same)

So there are multiple objects in the grid. You want to take the one that appears optically to appear on top of all the other ones from an illusion standpoint, as if it's shape is not being blocked by the other shapes in the grid. Whatever the shapes size is take the perimeter of it and make a square and duplicate it, with the blank boxes included in Black.

Look at the shapes presented on the input grid. There should be one shape that is complete (without being partially covered by another shape). Resize the grid to the dimensions of the complete shape, then replicate that shape in the same colour.
Take the shape that appears to be on the topmost order and use that as the solution. It's a bit tricky.

There should be a selection of shapes, The shape which has no other shapes overlapping it and is seemingly 'on top' of the other shapes is the one you want to choose. Change the grid size to be the same as the shape chosen and draw the shape in the grid
set grid to 4 x 4 and copy yellow object exactly

If the grid represents a 3D space and each color represents a single solid object, some objects are closer to the viewer than others, as though they have been stacked from front to back. One object can therefore be determined to be closest to the viewers than the others, determined by which object is least obscured by any other. Create a grid corresponding to this object's height and width and recreate the object within the grid.
Find the top layered shape in the original. The grid should be the same width and height as this shape. Now color the shape to be the same color and shape as that top layered shape in the original.

Imagine you are looking down from above at the shapes in the input. Reproduce only the shape that would be on the very top (highest up if looking straight down). Set the output grid to the dimensions of that shape and reproduce that shape only (keep color the same).
Create a grid that is 2 by 6. Fill it with yellow.

Look at the shapes shown. It should show overlapping shapes of different colours. Note which coloured shape is on top. Copy this shape, creating a grid that fits it as close as possible without deleting any of this shape. If it is an irregular shape or line that  is not the same shape as the grid, make sure that it is on a black background but the same colour as shown in the original picture.
find the shape that is not under any other of the shapes then make the grid size fit that shape then color that shape into the grid (keep colors the same)

Take the shape that "appears" to be on top with no other shapes covering it and make a grid that is as high and wide as only that shape. Copy the shape into the new grid exactly as it appears on the old one and in the same colour. Any "negative space" around that shape on the grid should be the same as it would be on the original, ie same colour etc.

There will be rectangle shapes of colors. It will appear these rectangles are stacked on top of each other. The one on top will be a full shape, covering part of the 2nd one, the 2nd one will be covering part of the 3rd one, and so forth. You need to pick the rectangle that is at the bottom of the stack (It will have part of it covered by all the other shapes). Resize the grid to the exact size of the shape on the bottom of the stack, then make all the squares the color of the shape on the bottom of the stack. Essentially, take the shape and the bottom of the stack and re-create it.
Look for the colored shape that appears to be all the way  behind the other shapes and create your grid based on that plus color it the same color.
Make a grid the size of the shape that is behind all of the others then use the same colour this is, to fill the grid

the shape that looks furthest to the "back" of the input grid is what will be put in the other grid. this is the shape that is covered the most by other shapes (so don't pick the one that isn't covered by anything else or the one that may be covered by only one other shape). make the grid the same size as this shape at the "back" and fill the grid in with only the colour of this shape.

The background object becomes the focal object and fills the gird in this task. Find the back-most object (the one covered by other colored objects that looks like its been cut off the most). Chage your grid side to correspond to the same height and width as this object (the number of squares in its height and width) Then fill this whole grid with the same color of the back-most object
Look at your grid and decide what the largest shape is, use those dimensions to create your grid, flood fill with the correct colour of the shape, submit.
The shapes will appear layered, one over the next. make the grid size the same as the bottom shape and recreate the shape in the grid so it fills it.
you are just focusing on the green object which is in effect underneath/behind the other objects.  use grid size 2x4 and colour block it all green

The input will contain three objects overlapping each other. Note which object is on the bottom layer. Use a grid the same size as the bottom object. Color the grid the same color as the bottom object.

There are multiple items in the input box. Find the item that looks like it is behind all the other items. Resize the grid to match the size of that item. Flood fill the grid with the same color as that item.
Identify the shape at the very back. Use the dimensions of that shape. Flood fill the colour of the shape.

Look at the input, there will be shapes stacked on top of each other. The answer is the shape and colour that is at the bottom of the stack. For the answer you need to make your grid size the same as the shape on the bottom of the stack and the colour of all the blocks is the same as the shape on the bottom of the stack.
Make the grid 3x3, the "bottom" shape is the one that becomes the solution so for this the bottom shape was a grey square which was 3x3 so once you've made it 3x3 just fill it grey.
Use the color that is running up and down and make it the foreground of the new grid.
make the grid the same size as the example. color the vertical top to bottom stripes in so it looks like they are in front and the side to side bars are behind them.
Make it so that the vertical lines are in the foreground.
the grid size remains the same as the original, copy the image from the input. change the vertical striped images to become the same colour vertically.

Use the same grid size as the size of the biggest object in the input. In your answers, wherever there is an overlapping between the two colors, the vertical block colors must always overlap the horizontal block colors. Keep all colors the same except when there is an overlapping then the overlapping block should take the color of the vertical block
With the vertical lines that are coloured, complete them so every square in that column is that colour. It does not matter if it crosses over the vertical lines
Change the input size to the one provided (13x8). Copy the image across but then change the colours so that the colour of the vertical lines is shown ahead of the horizontal lines.
copy grid size, design and colors. Except make all vericial lines the same color all the way up and down.
Use the same grid size.  There are horizontal and vertical objects.  At each intersection of the object lines change the color of the square to match the color of the vertical object.
Copy the pattern exactly.  Then focus only on the vertical line(s) of the same colour. Colour them in so they are all continuous line(s) from top to bottom with the same colour.

Resize the grid to the same size as the task input grid. Select the edit button and the appropriate colours. The rule is that the vertical blocks of colour must be a cointuous line of colour and not broken by the horizontal lines of colour.
Resize the grid and copy the shapes exactly. Now recolour so it looks like the vertical shapes are in front of the horizontal shapes.
Where the lines cross, change the colour of the LONG lines so they are unbroken in colour
Keep grid size the same as input, change all grid colours that go under to go over.
Copy the input image. Make all columns (that are any other colour than black) the same colour.
Resize the grid to the target image. Make sure the red parts don't go OVER the yellow. Yellow goes over Red.
Using the same grid and shapes, make it so that all of the vertical shapes are on top of the horizontal shapes
Change grid to example dimensions. No colors or sizes will be changed. All vertical lines will be placed on top of horizontal lines.
make the vertical stripes infront of horizonital ones

Copy from input. Any vertical lines should have precedence over any horizontal ones, for example the vertical lines should not be broken up by horizontal lines, as if the vertical lines are placed on top.
Reproduce the original grid and shapes exactly as they appear. Then make it appear as though the vertical lines go over the top of the horizontal lines. Keep the colours the same.

Identify the colour of all the vertical lines.
Fill all grid squares that intersect the horizontal zines with the colour of the vertical lines.
The vertical lines should be complete and not any other colour.

Keep the grid size and everything the same. It looks as if one colored line is crossing over the top of the other. Switch the colors of the one overlapping box, so it looks like the other line is on top now.

You should see two overlapping lines. All you need to do here is put the line which looks like it is front to the behind. It's a replica apart from that. If you can't understand that, essentially the cube where the two lines meet needs to change colour to the colour of the other line.
Keep the grid the same as the demonstration image. Invert the colour of the box (or boxes) that cross each other.
The rule is to recreate the input grid but to reverse any block colour where the lines overlap each other. I.E if the green overlaps the yellow change it for the yellow overlaps the green

Same grid size as your image. You may see two lines. Keep everything untouched in your image except change the one block where the two lines overlap into the other lines colour. Basically invert that one block into the other lines colour.
Fill in the vertical line all the way down until it is solid in colour, even if it cuts across the horizontal line
i copied the selection the filled in the missing part

Okay use the same grid size as the original and copy the original grid exactly as you see it using the same colors. Now you will need to change all the points where there lines cross each other to the opposite color shown in the original. So if you have a blue line going horizontally and red line going vertically and the blue line is "overlapping" the red line you need to make it so the red line is "overlapping" the blue line in the configuration by changing the overlapping square to the other color of the intersecting lines. You may only have one intersecting points or you may have more but you need to make sure all intersecting points are now the opposite "bottom" color in the new grid.
Change the overlap of the lines so that the line that is behind moves to the front and the line that is in the front moves back.
Copy Grid size, Alternate the colour of the Y axis line to the colour of the x axis line.

Use the same grid size as in the input.  There will be three objects in the input.  Copy from input and bring the vertical object to the front and place the top horizontal object behind it and the bottom horizontal object goes in front.  Keep the colors the same.

Objects must be transformed over to the new grid in the exact same positions as they were in the previous grid, any interlapping colours however must be changed at the point of meeting. For instance, if a horizontal blue line and a vertical red line meet and at the point of contact it is red making one solid red line, that singular block must be converted to blue.
copied the pattern and moved the background colours to the foreground and the foreground colours to the background

Resize the grid and copy the shapes exactly. Now recolour the squares so that the column / row is in front of the other shape. If there are two columns, recolour it so that they are either moved front or back (depending on their original position).

Copy input grid, and find where there are intersect/crossing points in the lines. Switch the colors at the intersect point. Ex. if there is a blue line and a red line, and the intersection point is blue, change it to red.
Just follow the instruction.
Copy the grid and find the line that is in the background and move it to the foreground.
Copy the exact grid size and copy the shapes and colors over exactly. In any section where the colors overlap in the original, reverse them ONLY on that overlapped portion.

You should see two intersecting lines. 

Change the color of the block where the lines intersect to the color of the line it is crossing.

If the lines intersect more than once, change both intersecting squares to separate colors..

There are lines which overlap. When they overlap some of the lines go behind while others go in front. Reverse this so that those which went behind now go in front while those which went in front now go behind.
Use the same grid size as in the input. Change the crossing line to the other colour line.
use same grid size, and replicate the shapes on the grid, but this time the color that was on the bottom when the 2 shapes meet will now be on the top (showing)

the black grid is identical in size.  the shapes are identical and in the same places on the grid.  they are all one colour which is determined by the colour of the object that appears to be closest to view - the one with the full shape showing
copy the original and re-paint the shape with the color of the small image in it.

Recreate the original image in size and colours. There will be multiple objects in two colours. Recolour the object(s) that are underlayed with the colour of the object(s) that overlay them to create a single unified shape in a single colour (that of the overlaying shape in the original).

There will be multiple objects on the grid. Copy the original image in size, shape and colour. One of the two colours will be on objects overlaying the other. Recolour the object that is underneath the same colour as the overlaying object to create a single shape in a single colour (that of the overlaying object).
copy the same shape but make the whole colour the same as the smaller shapes colour
copy the whole shape exactly the same size but use the smaller areas colour to fill the whole of the shape.
make the entire shape the colour of the shapes that are overlapping the others
make the grid the size of how many squareas are outside the main shape

In the input grid, you will see multiple objects of different sizes and colors. Some of the objects will appear to be 'on top' and some will appear to be 'underneath' the other objects. If it helps, imagine they are pieces of paper and you are stacking them in physical space, to determine which are 'on top of' the stack. First, resize your grid to match the full size of the input grid. Now, you will begin to copy the objects into the grid. Copy all objects, but ALL objects must be one color only - the color of the object that is 'on top' in the input grid. You will end up with a single, larger shape in the color of the topmost object in the input grid, as though you had outlined all objects together and filled in the outline with the color of the 'upper' shape.

Dimensions and shape size/position stay the exact same.

Select the color of the smaller square or squares, and flood fill the bigger square with that color.
Same dimensions and shape size. Flood fill the bigger block with the color of the smaller blocks.
Replicate the object on the answer and change the color to that of the larger object
Replicate the object to the answer and change the color to the larger object's color
Recreate the original image in the same sizes and colours. There will be multiple objects in two colours. Recolour the larger object in the same colour as the smaller obect(s).

Recreate the original image in the same size and colours. There will be multiple objects in 2 colours. Flood fill the larger object with the same colour as the smaller object to create one single object in a single colour.

Recreate the original image in size, shape and colour. There will be multiple objects on the grid. Some will overlay others. Flood fill the object(s) that are under the overlaying objects to create a single shape in a single colour (that of the overlaying object).

Use the same grid size as the input. The answer is the same shapes as the input. The colour of the shapes must all be the same colour. The required colour is the same colour of the shape that is touching the side/edge of the input.

Use the same grid size as the input. The shapes in the input are the same as the shapes in the answer. In the answer change the colour of all shapes to the same colour as the colour of the smallest shape or shapes.

the grid size remains the same.  the objects are the same size but are all the same colour.  the colour is the colour of the object which appears closest to view. - the one you can see the whole shape of.

In the input grid, you will see multiple objects of different colors and sizes. Some objects will be 'over' or covering part of the other objects. (Imagine they are pieces of colored paper, cut out and laid over one another if this helps.) First, resize your grid to match the input grid. Now, begin to copy ALL objects into the grid in their exact positions. However, you will not be copying the COLORS on the input grid. You will be changing the colors of the objects so that ALL objects are the same color as the "top" object (or objects, the ones that would be laid on top if it were pieces of paper), so that when you are finished, you will have a single, larger object in your grid, that is the same color as the topmost layer object in the input grid. It will be as though you outlined all the objects in the input grid, then filled the outline with the color of the uppermost object.

In the input grid, you will see multiple, different objects of different colors and sizes. Some will be "layered" over the other objects (imagine they are cutout pieces of paper if you need to, then you will see that some will be laid on top of others). For the sake of the description, we will call the ones laid on top of others the "upper" objects. First, copy the size of the input grid. Now, begin to copy the objects onto the grid. You will copy all of the objects, in their exact grid locations. However, you will need to color ALL of the objects, regardless of their colors in the input grid, the same color as the "upper" object(s) in the input grid. When you are finished, your final piece will appear as though you traced the outline of all objects together, and then filled the outline with the color of the upper object(s).

In the input grid you will see several different sized, different colored objects. Some objects will be "laid on top of" the other objects, if you imagine them in physical space (or Photoshop layers). First, copy the size and shape of the input grid. Now begin copying the objects in their exact location and size into your grid. The only thing you will change is the color - ALL objects must be the same color as the 'top layer' objects in the input grid.

In the input, you will see several objects. Some will be layered 'on top' of others (imagine they are paper, or Photoshop layers). First, copy the size and shape of the input grid. Now, you can hit "copy from input" to copy the shapes over exactly in their same size and location. BEFORE YOU SUBMIT IT, you will need to change the color. Change the color of all objects to the color of the 'uppermost' objects in the input. All objects in your final shape should be a single color and one contiguous shape.

Grid dimensions and shape position/size are the exact same.

You must flood-fill the color of the bigger square with the color of the smaller square/squares. This means everything is one big color.
Dimensions and shape size are the same. Flood fill the bigger block with the color of the smaller blocks.
re-copy the original image and color the whole shape with the color of the small box.
copy the original and re-color the whole shape with the color of the small shape in there.
copy the original image and re-color the whole shape with the color of the small shape.
repeat the original image and color the shape with the color of the small shape inside.
repeat the original and color it with the color of the small shape in there.
There will be objects of two colours on the grid. Resize the grid to 12x12. Create the original objects in the same colours. Flood fill the object that is larger with the smaller objects colour.

Use the same grid size as the input. The shapes in the input are the same as the answer. For the answer, change the colour of all of the shapes to the same colour as the colour of the smallest shape or shapes.

Use the same grid size as the input. The shapes in the input are the same as the shapes in the answer and haven't moved. All of the shapes must change colour to be the same colour as the smallest shape or shapes from the input.
